title: Cooking Oil on Cotton
timeEstimate: 10–30 minutes (fresh); aged oil climbs a tier
stainChemistry: Cooking oil (vegetable, olive, canola, soybean) is long-chain triglyceride oil. Low viscosity, penetrates cotton rapidly. Fresh oil is easily emulsified by surfactant (Dawn-class detergents specifically target triglycerides). Aged oil is much harder because polyunsaturated oils (canola, soybean, corn) OXIDIZE OVER TIME into a cross-linked polymer film — the same chemistry that produces linseed-oil varnish. Heat dramatically accelerates that polymerization; a cotton shirt with a fresh oil stain that goes through a hot dryer can come out with a semi-permanent shiny film. Cotton tolerates NSD, POG, mineral spirits, and hot wash at room temperature.
whyThisWorks: Cornstarch / baking soda preload pulls the bulk of fresh oil UP out of the fiber through capillary action — no chemistry needed. Dawn or NSD then handles whatever remains. For aged / polymerized oil, a solvent pass (mineral spirits or POG) is needed BEFORE the detergent step because hardened film doesn't emulsify. Hot wash finishes.
defaultAssumption: Fresh cooking oil on cotton is a high-confidence DIY — cornstarch + Dawn clears most cases in 10 minutes. Aged or hot-dried oil trends harder: the polyunsaturated ones (canola, soybean) cross-link into varnish and need solvent pre-treatment.

spottingProtocol.technique: Cornstarch / baking soda wicks oil up
spottingProtocol.equipment: Cornstarch, baking soda, or fuller's earth
spottingProtocol.instruction: Cover the stain completely with cornstarch, baking soda, or fuller's earth. Thick layer, not a dusting. 15–30 min minimum, longer on heavier stains. Powder wicks triglyceride UP from the fiber through capillary action without any moisture. Brush off with a soft brush. 50–70% of fresh oil leaves in this step.

spottingProtocol.equipment: NSD in warm water, OR Dawn + warm water (home)
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply surfactant directly to the stain, work with a soft bristle brush for 1–2 minutes. Dawn is engineered specifically for triglyceride emulsification. Rinse cool from the back.

spottingProtocol.technique: POG or mineral spirits for set film
spottingProtocol.equipment: POG or mineral spirits on clean white cloth
spottingProtocol.instruction: For aged or hot-dried oil that's formed a shiny, stiff film: tamp POG or mineral spirits on a white cloth from the back. Solvent dissolves the polymerized film back to liquid oil, which the NSD can then emulsify. Ventilate. Fresh oil doesn't need this step.
After any solvent pass, re-apply NSD/Dawn to emulsify the oil the solvent freed. Rinse cool.
Launder hot per care label. Inspect BEFORE the dryer — any residual oil polymerizes under dryer heat.

steps: 1. Cornstarch / fuller's earth preload 15–30 min. Brush off.
3. Aged / polymerized only: POG or mineral spirits on cloth, tamp from back.
4. Repeat NSD to emulsify newly-freed oil.
professionalProtocol.temperature: room temp through spotting; hot wash post-chemistry
warnings: Never hot-dry residual oil — polymerizes to a semi-permanent film
Fresh oil doesn't need solvent — don't over-treat

homeSolutions.instruction: Cover the oil stain with a thick layer of cornstarch, baking soda, or even plain white flour. Don't brush it in — just pour it on. Let it sit 15–30 minutes. The powder wicks the oil UP out of the fabric through capillary action. Brush off with a soft brush and repeat if the stain is still visibly wet. For fresh oil, this step alone clears most of it.

Work a generous amount of Dawn dish soap directly into the stain with a soft toothbrush or fingertip — more than you'd use on most stains. Dawn is chemistry-engineered for oils; it's what wildlife rehabilitators use to clean oil-spill birds. Let it sit 5 minutes, rinse warm. Repeat if any color / sheen remains.

If the oil has been sitting for days or has already been through a dryer, it may have polymerized into a shiny, stiff film. Soap alone won't touch it. Tamp mineral spirits (paint thinner — hardware store) on a white cloth onto the spot from the back, then go back to the Dawn step. Ventilate; no flames.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Mineral spirits are flammable. Open a window, no smoking, no candles.

homeSolutions.agent: Hot Wash, Inspect Before the Dryer
homeSolutions.instruction: Launder on the hottest setting the care label allows. Inspect BEFORE the dryer — any residual oil polymerizes under dryer heat into a semi-permanent film. If you still see a greasy spot, repeat the Dawn pass first.

If the oil has been through a hot dryer and has a shiny film, or two home rounds didn't clear it, take to a dry cleaner. Tell them: "Cooking oil on cotton, age [fresh / overnight / aged, polymerized, hot-dried], home-treated with cornstarch + Dawn + [mineral spirits]."

steps: 1. Cornstarch / baking soda / flour preload, 15–30 min. Brush off.
2. Dawn + warm water, generous amount, 5 min, rinse. Repeat.
3. Aged / polymerized: mineral spirits on a white cloth from the back (ventilate). Then Dawn again.
4. Hot wash per care label. Inspect BEFORE the dryer.
5. Escalate if polymerized by heat and two rounds failed.
products: Cornstarch, baking soda, or white flour
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if the oil has been hot-dried into a shiny film AND two home rounds didn't clear it.
warnings: Powder preload first — saves half the work
Inspect BEFORE the dryer — dryer heat polymerizes residual oil permanently
Fresh oil doesn't need solvent — start simple

neverDo: Never hot-dry a garment with residual oil — polymerizes to semi-permanent
Never use solvent near open flame
Never skip the powder preload on larger stains
Never pour solvent — tamp with cloth
fiberSensitivities: Cotton tolerates NSD, Dawn, mineral spirits, POG, hot wash
Dyed cotton: solvent usually safe; inside-seam-test if concerned

materialWarnings: Powder preload cuts the work in half
Inspect BEFORE the dryer — heat polymerizes oil

escalation.when: Oil has been hot-dried and polymerized into a shiny film, or two home rounds didn't clear it.
escalation.whatToTell: "Cooking oil on cotton, age [fresh / aged / hot-dried polymerized], home-treated with cornstarch + Dawn + [mineral spirits]."

contextQuestions: Has it been through a hot wash or dryer?
Type of oil — saturated (coconut, palm) or polyunsaturated (canola, soybean)? (polyunsaturated polymerizes faster)
Is the cotton white, light, or dark/dyed?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: The polymerization-under-heat pathway is the main home-treatment failure. Fresh oil is easy; fresh-oil-then-hot-dryer is MUCH harder. Catching oil before a dryer cycle is the practical make-or-break.
